Introduction
Lower limb orthoses serve multiple purposes, such as reducing 
pain in arthritic joints, improving gait, and controlling abnormal 
movements. They can be composed of a variety of materials, such 
as plastics, metal, and leather. They may or may not have joints to 
surround and support anatomic joints, such as ankles and knees. 
Orthoses come in prefabricated varieties but also can be created in 
a custom fashion to fit the unique needs of any given patient. 
Some general principles apply to their application with patients. 
Prescription should be based on careful assessment of the person’s 
particular disorder with a specific goal in mind. The fit should be 
comfortable with the least amount of restriction to accomplish 
the purpose(s) of the orthosis. When determining whether an off-
the-shelf orthosis vs. custom orthosis is appropriate, the biggest 
factor is not cost. The best determinants are functional outcome 
and length of need. If a patient needs the orthosis for only a short 
period (#6 months), then a noncustom orthosis may be appro -
priate. However, if the patient has a pathology that is going to 
require an orthosis for more than 6 months, a custom orthosis is 
likely the better solution for both fit and function. By placing the 
needs and functional goals of the patient as a priority and using 
the clinical expertise of the team members (physician, therapist, 
and orthotist), a great outcome is possible. 41
The three-point system of pressure applies to joints such as 
knees where two points of pressure are applied posteriorly and one 
anteriorly (Fig. 13.1). An orthotic knee joint should have an axis 
coincident with the medial femoral condyle, and for the ankle it 
should be over the tip of the medial malleolus. Hip joints should 
allow the patient to sit at 90 degrees. Lower limb orthoses should 
permit good function through all phases of gait. 35
Some definitions may be helpful for the subsequent sections. 
Coxa vara refers to a hip deformity, whereby the angle between 
the femoral head-neck span and the shaft is reduced to less than 
120 degrees. Coxa valga refers to an increase in this angle. Genu 
varum is commonly referred to as bow-leggedness. With respect 
to the thigh, the leg angulates medially. Genu valgum is com -
monly referred to as knock-knee, where the leg angulates laterally. 
Pes valgus occurs when the medial longitudinal arch collapses, 
and there is outward turning of the heel. Pes varus is associated 
with inward turning of the heel. Orthoses are named for the joint 
they cross over. An ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) crosses the ankle 
and foot. A knee-ankle-foot orthosis (KAFO) crosses the knee, 
ankle, and foot. A hip-knee-ankle-foot orthosis (HKAFO) crosses 
the hip, knee, ankle, and foot.
Shoes
Shoes have been worn for thousands of years to protect the feet 
from injury and environmental hazards. In the absence of abnor -
mal anatomy or pathology, a shoe is not needed to provide sup -
port to the normal foot. A shoe needs to be of the proper fit to 
prevent skin problems from arising. A callus is a thickening of the 
skin and/or soft tissue as a result of friction, which can result from 
shoes that are too loose. A corn is a type of callus that develops as 
a result of friction over bony prominences, such as the dorsal as -
pect of the toes, and is seen when a shoe is too tight.
The material used to make a shoe is also important. Leather 
has traditionally been a good material for most activities because 
it is pliable enough to conform to the feet as the shoes are worn 
over time while remaining durable and ventilating well. A pliable 
sole helps avoid interference with the natural biomechanics of 
• Technology allows patients with spinal cord injuries and hemi-
plegia to stand and ambulate.
• Assistive devices are an essential part of allowing patients to 
gain independence safely.
• Pediatric patients are not small adults; they have special consid -
erations for rapid changes and require more frequent follow-up 
by the rehabilitation team.
13
Lower Limb Orthoses
DOUGLAS P. MURPHY, WILLIAM LOVEGREEN, AND MARY T. LOVEGREEN
• Lower extremity orthoses provide biomechanical support for 
patients’ legs and lower trunk, allowing them to stand and 
ambulate or to prevent further joint damage.
• Ground reaction forces are utilized from patients’ shoes to their 
hip joints.
• Some lower extremity orthoses are utilized to prevent injury or 
further joint damage.
SUMMARY PEARLS

SECTION 2  Treatment Techniques and Special Equipment
foot motion. A wide variety of materials are used in the manufac-
ture of modern footwear.
Shoe Parts
Although there are a multitude of shoes on the market, two types  
of dress shoes, the Blucher and the Bal, are commonly worn  
(Fig. 13.2). The main distinguishing factor between these two shoe 
styles is whether the throat is open or not. In the Bal shoe, the quar-
ters (the upper portion of the shoe that wraps around the proximal 
portion of the foot and allows entry into the shoe) meet at the throat 
and are stitched to the tongue. In the Blucher shoe, the quarters do 
not meet, leaving the throat open for more movement of the tongue 
of the shoe. This allows the shoe to open more and better accom-
modate the foot in combination with an orthosis.
The toe box is the most anterior portion of the shoe and is fol-
lowed by the vamp, which holds the forefoot, then followed by the 
quarters posterior to that. The heel counter is the most posterior 
portion of the quarters and helps support the back of the shoe, 
which serves to help control the entirety of the foot (see Fig. 13.2).
Foot Orthoses
There are a wide range of different foot orthoses on the market. 
They may be bought over the counter or custom manufactured by 
an orthotist, but whether it is effective in treating its target ailment 
depends on correctly identifying the underlying problem. Using 
the most appropriate material and correctly molding the material 
to the patient’s foot are equally important. Over-the-counter or -
thoses are often sufficient for minor conditions. Custom-molded 
orthoses may be more effective for certain foot conditions 34 and 
are typically used for more severe or chronic cases. Over-the-
counter orthoses are commonly made of soft materials, whereas 
those custom-molded by an orthotist are more likely to be

semirigid to provide shock absorption and additional support to 
the foot. Rigid orthoses are used only for deformities that require 
aggressive bracing to attain sufficient control.
Foot orthoses work in part by altering the ground reactive 
forces acting on the joints in the lower limb and by affecting ro -
tational components of gait ( Fig. 13.3). Before casting a custom 
foot orthosis, the subtalar joint should be in a neutral position. 
Subtalar neutral has traditionally been considered the ideal foot 
alignment76 because it minimizes foot and ankle positional abnor-
malities, such as hyperpronation. This position helps treat such 
conditions as pes planus, patellofemoral pain syndrome, and 
painful rheumatoid arthritis of the first metatarsophalangeal 
joint.33,83
Common Foot Conditions
Pes Planus
Pes planus, commonly known as flat feet, is exceedingly common, 
with one study estimating a prevalence at 26.6% in adults. 75

257Chapter 13 Lower Limb Orthoses
Pes planus is seen with excessive pronation, or in-rolling, of the 
foot, which manifests as lowering of the medial aspect of the foot 
and elevating the lateral aspect. Foot pronation is a component of 
foot eversion. In addition to pronation at the subtalar joint, the 
motion of eversion also includes dorsiflexion of the ankle joint 
and forefoot abduction at the tarsometatarsal junction. Pes planus 
may be caused by increased internal rotation of the tibia (contrib-
uting to foot pronation) or abnormal alignment of the calcaneus. 
The interaction between the tibia and the subtalar joint is what 
allows nonintrinsic foot abnormalities to cause excessive prona -
tion of the foot.
Excessive pronation of the foot leads to altered distribution of 
forces and potentially to pain. Pain relief may be achieved by 
holding the subtalar joint and calcaneus in proper alignment, 
thereby reducing the degree of pronation. The subtalar joint 
should be held in a neutral position for the custom-molding pro -
cess. This prevents rotational deformities present in hyperprona -
tion or supination (see Fig. 13.3C and D).
With the orthosis elevating the anteromedial calcaneus, an 
upward force is transmitted to the sustentaculum tali, thereby 
preventing in-rolling. 20 By extending the orthosis beyond the 
metatarsal heads, one obtains increased leverage to control the

foot deformity. The orthosis should also extend proximally to cup 
the calcaneus and help to control the subtalar joint. A custom-
molded foot orthosis of this type, designed to control excessive 
pronation, is deemed a UCBL, or UCB, orthosis, named after the 
University of California Biomechanics Laboratory, where this 
design was researched during the 1940s.
In cases where ligamentous laxity of the foot is causing the pes 
planus, a medial longitudinal arch support is useful. If the initial 
arch support is too high, pain may increase, so a stepwise increase 
in arch height may improve tolerance of the orthosis and patient 
adherence. Extending the heel medially (known as a Thomas heel 
extension) may offer additional medial support, especially in pa -
tients with a high body mass index.
Useful advice for runners with pes planus or excessive prona -
tion of the foot is to use running shoes with a firm medial heel 
counter and a wide last at the shank (see Fig. 13.2), both of which 
serve to reduce pronation at the subtalar joint.
Pes Cavus
In contrast to pes planus, pes cavus is an abnormally high-arched 
foot, which can cause pain as a result of excess pressure placed on 
the metatarsal heads. T o prevent this, weight bearing should be 
distributed equally across the metatarsal heads. Additionally, in -
creasing the height of the longitudinal support of the shoe be -
tween the shank of the shoe and the arch of the foot serves to 
better distribute weight to help prevent excess pressure over the 
heads of the metatarsals. A pes cavus foot tends not to pronate like 
a pes planus foot, so the arch of the foot is located at the talona -
vicular joint. A foot may appear high arched if the tibia is exter -
nally rotated and the foot is supinated. A custom foot orthosis 
that holds the subtalar joint in neutral position helps prevent in -
ordinate supination and prevents increased pressure being exerted 
on the lateral aspect of the foot.
Forefoot Pain (Metatarsalgia)
As mentioned, to help relieve pain on the metatarsal heads, 
modifications must be made to distribute pressure to other areas. 
Internal or external modifications can be used to redistribute 
forces to other areas, typically proximal to the heads of the 
metatarsal bones. A metatarsal pad is placed inside the shoe just 
proximal to the heads of the second through fourth metatarsals. A 
metatarsal bar can also be used when the foot is unable to tolerate 
a pad within the shoe. This is a wide band of material applied 
externally to the sole of the shoe just proximal to the metatarsal 
heads. A rocker bottom shoe helps decrease force on the metatar-
sals during push-off during the gait cycle by allowing the body’s 
center of mass to passively roll over the base of support rather than 
requiring active ankle plantarflexion.57 Shoes with high heels and/
or pointed toes place excessive stress on the metatarsal heads.  
It is important to educate patients about this fact because selec -
tion of appropriate footwear can help to prevent the development 
of pain.
Heel Pain
Orthoses can help to distribute weight to avoid painful areas of 
the heel. In relatively minor cases, a heel pad placed inside the 
shoe can be sufficient. In chronic cases where the foot is unable to 
tolerate a pad inside the shoe, a calcaneal bar is placed distal to the 
calcaneus to prevent it from assuming full weight during stance

phase. Alternatively, this can be accomplished using a shoe with a 
spring for a heel, also set anterior on the calcaneus. Rocker bot -
tom shoes cause heel strike and the subsequent ground reaction 
force (GRF) to be directed anterior to the calcaneus.
Plantar fasciitis is a disorder of the fibrous tissue along the 
medial aspect of the sole of the foot that commonly causes medial 
heel pain where the fascia attaches. Hyperpronation of the foot 
places increased stress on the medial longitudinal arch. Similar to 
cases of pes planus, a custom orthosis that holds the subtalar joint 
in a neutral position serves to reduce pronation and thus stress on 
the medial arch. The shoe should use a firm medial heel counter 
with a wide shank.
High arches also predispose to development of plantar fasciitis 
caused by the increased stress on the medial arch during stance 
phase. Adding arch support and redistributing pressure along the 
length of the arch serves to reduce heel pain.
Heel spurs in plantar fasciitis arise from the increased me -
chanical stress of the plantar fascia on its attachment on the cal -
caneus. However, the spurs themselves are not necessarily the 
source of the pain because they are frequently seen in asymptom-
atic individuals.6
Plantar fascia night splints consist of an AFO placed in a few 
degrees of dorsiflexion.80 While the patient is sleeping, the AFO 
passively stretches the plantar fascia. This can be used for as  
long as the patient is symptomatic because these splints carry  
very minimal if any risk, although relief may be seen as early as  
1 to 3 months in nonrefractory cases. 48
Heel lifts serve to decrease tension on the Achilles tendon by 
keeping the ankle slightly plantar flexed. Achilles enthesitis can be 
treated with a heel lift used over a period of weeks but should not 
be used for months because of the risk of developing a plantarflex-
ion contracture. Paradoxically, when plantar flexor spasticity or 
contracture is already present, a heel lift helps increase the heel 
height and promote heel strike before toe touch when entering 
the stance phase during the gait cycle.
Toe Pain
Common causes of toe pain include gout, arthritis, and hallux 
rigidus (loss of normal mobility of the toe joint caused by arthri -
tis). Orthoses serve to reduce pain from these issues by immobi -
lizing the toes. Reducing mobility of the toe joints may be  
accomplished through use of a carbon insert extending along  
the full length of the shoe. If already using a metal AFO, the  
steel shank may be extended forward to accomplish the same  
effect.
Leg Length Discrepancy
There are two types of leg length discrepancies: true leg length 
discrepancy and apparent leg length discrepancy. A true leg length 
discrepancy may result from an acute trauma, such as a femur 
fracture, or after a hip or knee replacement surgery. T o obtain this 
measurement, with the patient supine on the exam table, measure 
from the distal tip of the anterior superior iliac spine to the distal 
tip of the medial malleolus. An apparent leg length discrepancy 
may be seen when the true leg length is equal, but anatomic fac -
tors such as muscle imbalance, scoliosis, or pelvic fracture create a 
pelvic obliquity. This measurement is obtained by measuring from 
a midline landmark, generally either the umbilicus or pubic sym-
physis, to the distal tip of the medial malleolus. Leg length can 
also be assessed radiographically.
Ninety percent of the population has some degree of leg length 
discrepancy, with an average discrepancy of 5.2 mm.44 This is of-
ten asymptomatic with smaller differences, but a difference in leg 
length of greater than half an inch is generally considered clini -
cally significant. There is a higher incidence of low back pain 
when the discrepancy is greater than 12 mm (or approximately 
half an inch).82 If pain improves after a trial of shoe modifications, 
then the leg length discrepancy can be reasonably assumed to have 
been a contributing factor.
If the leg length discrepancy is greater than half an inch, the 
first half inch may be corrected with a heel pad; anything more 
than this requires external buildup of the heel. Even then, only 
75% of the discrepancy should be corrected. The sole of the rest 
of the shoe must be built up in proportion to the heel.
The Diabetic Foot
The key to treating the diabetic patient who has neuropathy is 
prevention of any type of foot problems. The biggest cause of foot 
problems is ill-fitting shoes causing breakdown to the plantar sur-
face of the foot and distal portion of the toes. Impaired sensation 
and the patient’s lack of follow-up and doing frequent skin-foot 
checks is a recipe for foot ulcer(s) and subsequent amputations.9
Diabetic shoes have a higher and wider toe box and an interior 
surface free of any seams or irregularities. They also have insoles 
that consist of multilayer, multidense materials that reduce shear 
forces and pointed pressure areas. For more complex diabetic foot 
problems, custom shoes and/or custom foot orthoses can be made.
Osteoarthritis of the Knee
Although osteoarthritis of the knee is not a primary foot condi -
tion, foot orthoses have been used to alter the GRFs affecting the 
more proximal joints of the lower limb. For instance, if medial 
compartment joint space narrowing is present, a lateral heel 
wedge changes the GRF and attempts to offload the medial com-
partment of the knee. However, current meta-analyses suggest 
that this intervention is not effective at reducing medial knee pain 
caused by osteoarthritis.72,94
Pediatric Shoes
A simple design is best for pediatric footwear. Soft soles allow the 
feet to develop naturally, and an elevated heel should not be pres-
ent, so as to facilitate a normal gait. For most children, a tennis 
shoe is sufficient. A high-cut shoe will stay in place better than a 
low-cut shoe, especially for very young children. Shoes will need 
to be changed frequently in the first few years of a child’s life be-
cause of rapid growth.
Flat feet are commonly seen in infants and children. 85 They 
may also occur in asymptomatic adults without the presence of 
pathology. As subcutaneous fat is lost and joint laxity decreases as 
the child matures, flat feet tend to improve over time. Overall, 
current evidence indicates that foot orthoses are beneficial in the 
treatment of symptomatic pediatric flexible pes planus, but cau -
tion should still be exercised because of a lack of standardized 
parameters and methodological concerns in some studies. 18
Ankle-Foot Orthoses
AFOs are used for problems dealing with foot and ankle issues 
that are either biomechanical or neurophysiological in nature.

259Chapter 13 Lower Limb Orthoses
These orthoses include any device that passes across the ankle 
joint but stays distal to the knee joint. Such orthoses are used for 
diagnoses that range from ankle sprains to stroke management. 
This type of brace could potentially be used for any neuromuscu-
lar condition requiring support or assistance at the level of the 
foot and ankle. Of all the various types of lower limb orthoses, 
AFOs receive the most prescriptions.
Ankle and foot motions are complex. AFOs provide dorsiflex-
ion and plantarflexion control but also provide mediolateral sta -
bility. Motions at the tibiotalar joint (ankle joint) or subtalar joint 
and motions of the forefoot at the tarsometatarsal joints combine 
to provide either eversion or inversion. Eversion consists of ankle 
dorsiflexion, subtalar pronation, and forefoot abduction. Inver -
sion reverses these motions for ankle plantarflexion, subtalar supi-
nation, and forefoot adduction. AFOs provide direct control of 
ankle motions but also influence control of the knee during gait. 
Consequently, AFOs can beneficially influence problems with 
various types of ankle weakness that range from ankle flaccidity to 
focused areas of weakness, such as with a lack of ankle dorsiflexion 
that causes foot drag during swing-through in the gait cycle. 
These orthoses also provide control for spasticity and contrac -
tures. Painful ankles, such as from arthritic conditions, can be 
offloaded to some extent, and wounds such as diabetic plantar 
wounds can be protected. Examples of conditions that have im -
proved through the incorporation of AFOs include stroke, cere -
bral palsy, spina bifida/myelodysplasia, mononeuropathies and 
polyneuropathies, spinal cord injury (SCI) such as in incomplete 
paraplegia, motor neuron disease in the earlier stages, among oth-
ers.14 Gait issues, such as hyperextension of the knee or genu re -
curvatum, can be beneficially influenced through mechanisms 
that will subsequently be described.
When the foot strikes the ground, a GRF is exerted against the 
foot in a vector direction opposite to the striking foot. 90 The di-
rection of this vector can fall behind the knee, and in this case the 
GRF will tend to encourage flexion of the knee (Fig. 13.4). If the 
GRF falls in front of the knee, then knee extension is assisted. By 
adjusting certain parameters, an AFO can create either one of 
these GRFs. Dorsiflexion of the foot tends to create a GRF that is 
posterior to the knee and enhances knee flexion, while plan -
tarflexion augments knee extension. AFOs with ankle joints can 
have plantarflexion stops or dorsiflexion stops that encourage the 
desired action of the GRF on the knee. For example, in someone 
with weak quadriceps with a tendency to have the knee collapse 
during gait from weakness, the dorsiflexion stop can be adjusted 
toward greater plantarflexion during the stance phase. Plantarflex-
ion will move the GRF anterior to the axis of the knee and pro -
vide a force to keep the knee more extended. Thus too much 
flexion of the knee with possible collapse will be discouraged. 
Conversely, in an individual with a knee that hyperextends during 
stance, the plantarflexion stop can be adjusted to keep the ankle 
more dorsiflexed. This adjustment will move the GRF posterior to 
the knee joint and encourage more flexion and discourage the 
hyperextension of the knee that was occurring previously. Ideally, 
these devices would facilitate all aspects of gait; however, in prac -
tice, correction of one issue can leave other issues less well at -
tended to. For example, rigid braces designed to protect healing 
pathology such as fractures or wounds will create more difficulties 
in walking and require a greater energy expenditure. Another ex -
ample is the Intrepid Dynamic Exoskeletal Orthosis ( Fig. 13.5). 
This device helps reduce pain and facilitate running for those with 
limb salvage, but the very features that enhance running make 
walking more difficult.36
Metal Ankle-Foot Orthosis
Metal AFOs are composed of metal uprights, a foot plate or other 
interface between the brace and the shoe, and leather straps  
(Fig. 13.6). The ankle joints of these types of braces have pins or 
springs for adjustments that are adapted to various pathologies and 
needs (Fig. 13.7). The metal in the posterior area of the calf band

The top of the calf band should rest at least 1 inch below the fibu-
lar neck to avoid compression on the peroneal nerve.64
The interface with the shoe consists of either a plate (see  
Fig. 13.6) with medial and lateral uprights for the ankle joints or 
a split stirrup that fits into the side of the shoe. The single plate 
is fixed firmly to the shoe and can extend to the metatarsal heads

The pins on the joints can be tightened or loosened to provide 
restriction to dorsiflexion or plantarflexion. There are anterior and 
posterior stops on the medial and lateral joints. The anterior stop 
limits dorsiflexion, while the posterior one limits plantarflexion. 
Straps can be attached near the joints to limit either a tendency 
toward varus or valgus. A medial T strap will apply pressure over 
the medial malleolus and buckle around the lateral upright. A 
lateral T strap will apply pressure over the lateral malleolus and 
buckle around the medial upright.
In general, metal AFOs have been replaced by plastic and other 
materials, but metal AFOs still have utility and are prescribed in 
certain circumstances. For example, an individual may have

grown accustomed to a metal brace and thus despite the advan -
tages of newer types may not want to change. In other instances, 
the weight of an individual may require the sturdiness that a metal 
AFO can provide. Skin conditions such as sores, weeping, or exu-
dative lesions can necessitate the use of metal AFOs as well.
Plastic Ankle-Foot Orthosis
Plastic AFOs (Fig. 13.9) come in a wide variety of styles and are 
composed of different types of materials. Examples of plastic 
AFOs are the solid ankle, posterior leaf spring, hinged, spiral, 
hemispiral, Swedish, energy return, and short-leg fixed AFOs. 
Most plastic AFOs are made of polypropylene. Some are hybrids 
of this material and a canvas fabric. A lesser number of orthoses 
are made with copolymer. Soft foam or plastic can be combined 
with a harder, more durable shell. These types of devices may or 
may not include medial and lateral joints with anterior and pos -
terior stops similar in function to those mentioned for metal 
AFOs. These joints can be single or double action and may have 
spring-assisted dorsiflexion to assist with toe clearance during 
swing phase. The position of trimlines either around the ankle or 
the foot can be varied as necessary so that if more support is nec -
essary the trimline can be extended. The length of the foot plate 
can vary from proximal to distal to the metatarsal heads.
Other Types of Ankle-Foot Orthoses
Although plastic AFOs are the most common, other variations 
have been tried and are in use. Examples include carbon fiber and 
laminated AFOs. The laminated AFOs are made in a way that is 
similar to the way laminated sockets are made for prostheses. 
Hybrids have been created, such as plastic and carbon fiber, plastic 
and metal, and carbon fiber and metal. One example is the Blue 
Rocker AFO81 (Fig. 13.10), which is a carbon composite. This is 
designed for foot drop with associated flaccidity or varying levels

263Chapter 13 Lower Limb Orthoses
Knee Orthoses
General Considerations
Knee orthoses (KOs) are a very commonly prescribed class of 
brace both because of the high prevalence of knee osteoarthritis 
and the high rate of knee injuries in recreational and professional 
athletes.1,17,32,50,61 KOs are designed to provide stability, prevent 
instability, limit motion, and/or provide offloading of the knee 
joint. Most include both a medial and lateral upright with either 
a free motion or adjustable knee joint. A KO is primarily indi -
cated when the ankle and foot are fully functional, such that a 
KAFO is not required. The indications for provision of a KO are 
further delineated in Box 13.1.
Brace Design Features
Although many of the KOs have similar features, subtle differ -
ences in the materials used and design features have the potential 
to impact the fit, comfort, and function of the brace.5,55 Box 13.2 
highlights the important KO design features that should be taken 
into consideration to provide the most appropriate brace. Most 
KOs being prescribed are prefabricated-type braces. There are also 
a host of prefabricated KOs available commercially. Many of these 
off-the-shelf devices have some degree of adjustability for custom-
ization for the individual. Fully custom KOs are less frequently 
prescribed and used.
KOs have traditionally been fabricated with metal, plastic, and 
fabric materials. The materials used in sleeve-type KOs have evolved 
to provide enhanced compression, comfort, and support; improve 
donning; and reduce skin complications ( Fig. 13.13 ).29,55,62,84 
Braces have been increasingly manufactured with lightweight com-
posite materials, such as carbon fiber and aluminum alloy.5,29,62,84 
This has decreased the weight of the brace while maintaining dura-
bility and strength. Some brace fabrication concepts also take into 
consideration the importance of the skin interface with padding or 
foam inserts. The use of undersleeves or silicone liners can improve 
comfort and reduce migration, which helps with compliance.
Important considerations are the mechanism and materials 
used for suspension of the orthosis. Suspension of a KO can be 
challenging, especially for individuals with conical-shaped thighs 
or excessive soft tissues. Suspension relies upon the use of con -
toured uprights in conjunction with correctly placed strapping. 
Adequate sizing and appropriate tightening of straps are also im -
portant to decrease the potential for slippage. If needed in ex -
treme conditions, an AFO section can be attached to the KO for 
enhanced suspension and alignment.
Prescription Considerations
Prescription is complicated by the wide variety of KOs that are 
commercially available from a large number of different manufac-
turers. KOs range from simple pull-on knee sleeves to sophisticated 
braces with adjustable ROM limitations and offloading capabili -
ties.5,29 Different classification schemes have been used to classify 
KOs. These classification schemes may take into consideration 
whether the orthosis is prefabricated, custom, or custom fit. Other 
classification schemes are based on whether the brace is designed to 
be supportive, corrective, or functional. Although some braces are 
designed to primarily serve one purpose, others have features that 
address several different functions. Box 13.3 lists some of the most 
commonly prescribed types of KOs.
Simple knee immobilizers have no joint and are designed to 
keep the knee in full extension after knee injury or surgical 
intervention. Sleeve-type KOs can be simple in design and pro -
vide mild compressive support to the knee, whereas others  
can incorporate articulated hinges for greater medial-lateral 
support. 62 Other sleeves are designed to specifically provide

265Chapter 13  Lower Limb Orthoses
Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthoses
General Considerations
KAFOs are designed to provide stability to the knee and ankle 
complex during ambulation. The KAFO may be required to pro-
vide stability during the loading response of the gait cycle by pre-
venting excessive knee flexion or during midstance to terminal 
stance by preventing knee hyperextension. Ideally, the KAFO pro-
vides the required stability while minimally interfering with normal 
knee and ankle ROM during the gait cycle. KAFOs can be pre -
scribed and used for functional ambulation, exercise, or both.
58,59 
For some individuals, the use of KAFOs is complemented by the 
use of a wheelchair for longer distance mobility. Good trunk con-
trol and upper body strength are typically required to ambulate 
with KAFOs because these devices are used in combination with 
such ambulation aids as walkers or forearm crutches.
Design Feature Considerations
Ankle Joints
Any style of ankle joint or ankle joint system available for use  
in AFOs (as previously described) can be incorporated into 
KAFOs.
27,58,59 KAFOs most commonly use a double upright 
design at the ankle, but a unilateral upright or a posterior strut 
without an ankle articulation can also be used. Double uprights 
attached with stirrups to the outside of the shoe accommodate 
edema and can be used with sensory impairment, but these styles 
can be heavy and make switching shoes difficult. Molded plastic 
ankle joints with or without an articulation allow the patient to 
switch shoes more easily and potentially provide more detailed 
control of the ankle and foot complex (Fig. 13.17).
• Fig. 13.17 Knee-ankle-foot orthosis with articulated thermoplastic an-
kle-foot orthosis component and posterior offset knee joint. (From Loveg-
reen W, Kwasniewski M, Panchang P , et al. Orthotic management of polio 
and postpolio syndrome. In: Webster JB, Murphy DP , eds. Atlas of Ortho-
ses and Assistive Devices. 5th ed. Elsevier; 2019:277-288.)
Knee Joints
There are three traditional types of knee joints.56,58,89 The straight, 
single-axis knee joint provides rotation about a single axis. It al-
lows free flexion but prevents knee hyperextension. Free-swinging 
knee joints are indicated when medial-lateral support is required, 
but adequate knee extensor strength is present. This type of knee 
joint can be used in combination with a drop lock if needed to 
keep the knee locked in extension during all phases of gait. The 
polycentric knee joint uses a double-axis system to simulate  
the flexion-extension and gliding motion of the femur across the 
tibia. Although this type of joint provides more physiological  
motion, it is not used as commonly as the other types of joints. 
The third type of knee joint is the posterior offset knee joint  
(Fig. 13.18). This joint allows free flexion and extension of the 
knee during the swing phase of gait. During stance phase, the 
posterior offset of the joint helps keep the orthotic GRF in front 
of the knee axis for stability. The posterior offset knee joint should 
have a hyperextension stop to help prevent genu recurvatum.
Knee Locks
Although the knee joint on a KAFO can be designed to be free 
swinging, knee locks are used to provide enhanced stability as 
indicated for the individual patient. The four traditional types of 
knee locks are the drop lock, ratchet lock, dial lock, and bail lock. 
More recently, various types of stance control and dynamic con -
trol locking mechanisms have been introduced. S tance control 
lock mechanisms are activated to lock during stance phase but 
allow knee motion (flexion and extension) to occur during swing 
phase.
27,58,89 Dynamic systems provide some level of knee motion 
control during both the stance and swing phases of gait through 
mechanisms such as pneumatic or hydraulic systems.
89
The drop lock provides a simple and lightweight design; how-
ever, fine motor coordination in the hands is required to lock and 
unlock the knee. Another disadvantage of the drop lock is that 
there is no locking mechanism until full knee extension is

obtained. A drop lock can be used with a single upright if the 
patient is relatively lightweight and has a low activity level.
The ratchet lock has a catching mechanism that operates in 
12-degree increments. As the user rises from a seated position, the 
ratchet lock prevents instability by blocking flexion and keeping 
the gains made toward extension. With the knee extended, knee 
flexion is achieved either by pressing down on a release lever or by 
sliding the locking mechanism open.
The dial lock is also used to stabilize the knee in varying 
amounts of flexion. It can be adjusted in 6-degree increments and 
is more precise than the ratchet lock for the management of a 
knee flexion contracture. Both the ratchet and the dial lock sys -
tems can be used to prevent progression of a flexion contracture 
or assist with the gradual reduction of a flexion contracture.
The bail lock provides an easier method of simultaneously 
unlocking the medial and lateral knee joints of a KAFO. Lifting 
the posteriorly located bail mechanism releases the knee joint to 
permit flexion, allowing the patient to sit down. The patient can 
also catch the bail on the edge of a chair to release the lock 
mechanism to permit sitting. The locking mechanism is spring 
loaded to assist locking the knee into full extension.
Stance control KAFOs use different mechanisms to lock and 
unlock the knee joint during the gait cycle. Some of these mecha-
nisms are weight activated, whereas others use different gait param-
eters, such as hip extension or ankle dorsiflexion, to release the lock 
mechanism.
71,89,92 These systems most commonly use full exten -
sion of the knee joint to initiate locking of the joint. The advantage 
of weight-activated systems is that some will lock in place to  
prevent knee flexion during weight bearing without full knee ex-
tension being achieved. Some of these stance control locking 
mechanisms are mechanical in nature, whereas others use magnetic 
or electronic switches (Fig. 13.19).
69 More advanced technologies 
use dynamic control of knee resistance throughout the gait cycle. 
The C-brace (Otto Bock) incorporates a microprocessor control 
system with a hydraulic joint. The hydraulics and microprocessor 
control allow flexion and extension dampening in both swing and 
• Fig. 13.19 E-Mag stance control knee-ankle-foot orthosis with electro -
magnetic knee control mechanism. (Courtesy Ottobock, Duderstadt, 
Germany.)
stance phases. The microprocessor control adjusts quickly to vary-
ing gait situations and sensor feedback, allowing the individual to 
more easily traverse inclines and stairs. 89 The C-brace also has a 
standing feature, which keeps the knee locked for prolonged stand-
ing. The benefits of stance control and dynamic knee control units 
include greater stability on uneven terrain and reduced metabolic 
costs of ambulation. Reducing gait deviations, such as hip hiking 
and circumduction, may also lead to a decrease in longer term 
complications in the back and contralateral limb.
Additional Component and Material Considerations
The materials selected for use in the KAFO will influence the 
strength, durability, flexibility, and weight of the brace. T radition-
ally, the materials used in KAFOs were metal (primarily steel) and 
leather. Although strong and durable, braces using these materials 
are heavy.
27,58,59,89 Currently, most KAFOs are made of more 
lightweight components consisting of aluminum, thermoplastics, 
and carbon fiber composite materials in combination with syn -
thetic fibers and Velcro straps.
71,89,92 Bands and/or shell structures 
are used at the thigh and the calf to secure the brace to the person 
and provide the control points for knee stability. The thigh band 
needs to be wide enough to adequately distribute the pressure of 
the forces transmitted through the knee axis. A partial thigh shell 
can provide a larger contact area and reduce the risk of high-
pressure areas on the skin. An additional thigh band located low 
on the thigh may be indicated to control genu recurvatum. In a 
similar manner, the calf component of the KAFO can consist of a 
padded band or a molded calf shell. Foam liners can be used to 
increase comfort with shell style braces.
Clinical Indications
As described, the purpose of a KAFO is to provide stability at the 
knee, ankle, and subtalar joints during ambulation. Clinical indi-
cations for a KAFO include lower extremity weakness involving 
the knee extensors and flexors, structural knee instability, varus or 
valgus deformity at the knee, knee flexion spasticity, or hyperex -
tension of the knee ( Box 13.4).
10,71 One of the biomechanical 
problems with traditional KAFOs that maintain full knee exten -
sion during gait is the effect it has on the normal gait pattern. 
Providing a person who has a very unstable knee during stance 
phase with a locked knee allows the person to stand and ambulate 
safely. However, having a locked knee during the swing phase cre-
ates a need for hip hiking, circumduction of the limb, and/or 
vaulting in on the contralateral side to advance the limb forward. 
These gait deviations can result in long-term musculoskeletal 
complications. Advances in stance control and dynamic knee

Hip Orthoses
Hip orthoses are used to provide support for patients with hip 
disorders or after hip surgeries. They can provide control in various 
planes of hip motion, including flexion-extension, abduction-ad -
duction, and internal-external rotation. They may be unilateral or 
bilateral. In cases where prefabricated orthoses are not sufficient, 
they may be custom molded to fit the patient. Common uses for 
hip orthoses include treating hip dislocation, hip dysplasia, or after 
hip replacement surgery (hip arthroplasty). By helping to position 
the femoral head in the acetabulum, they allow for healing or 
prevention of further damage to the joint. The hip orthoses cur-
rently in use have hip joints that use an adjustable multipositional 
lock for flexion-extension control, abduction-adduction control, 
or both (Fig. 13.20). If the pelvic and/or thigh section of the or -
thosis are loose or ill fitting, attempts to control a dislocating hip 
prosthesis are likely to fail. For this reason, ensuring a proper and 
complete fit is of utmost importance.
Hip-Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthoses
HKAFOs are normally KAFOs (usually bilateral) that are attached 
to a hip device (pelvic band, lumbar sacral orthoses [LSOs], or 
thoracic LSOs [TLSOs]) for medical conditions warranting  
hip control. Box 13.5 provides a list of possible indications for

common reasons for prescribing HKAFOs. HKAFOs are usually

types of HKAFOs that range from the conventional style of

Powered Exoskeletons
Powered exoskeletons provide the potential for individuals with 
various levels of SCI and similar conditions to achieve ambulation 
in the hospital, home, and community settings (Video 50.1). 30 
Powered exoskeletons are designated as Class II (intermediate risk) 
medical devices by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA). 
Various brands of powered exoskeletons have become commer -
cially available, and several are FDA approved for use in different 
environments. T o date, three of the four commercially available 
powered exoskeletons have received FDA marketing clearance for 
use in persons with SCI (ReWalk in 2014, Ekso and Indego in 
2016) (Fig. 13.27). The various brands serve a range of SCI levels. 
The Ekso GT (Ekso Bionics, California, USA) and the REX (REX 
Bionics, London, UK) are able to be used in individuals with cervi-
cal level injuries. Others, such as the ReWalk (ReWalk Robotics 
Inc., Yokneam Illit, Israel) and Indego (Parker Hannifin, Ohio, 
USA; indego.com), may be applicable for those with injuries below 
the T4 level. T riceps function is needed for crutch stability for all 
except the REX.30 The ReWalk was the first system to be cleared 
by the FDA for both personal and rehabilitation use in the United 
States. Recently, this company has developed a unilateral version 
that provides support and muscle reeducation and strengthening 
for post cerebrovascular accident hemiparesis rehabilitation.
A host of benefits have been described with the use of powered 
exoskeletons in individuals with SCI. 30,60,68,93 In selected in -
stances, these exoskeletal devices allow previously nonambulatory 
individuals to ambulate either independently or with supervision. 
• Fig. 13.27 ReWalk powered exoskeleton. (Courtesy ReWald Robotics, 
Marlborough, MA.)
Achieving ambulation in individuals with SCI improves their 
level of physical activity and cardiovascular fitness, and research is 
ongoing about potential short- and long-term health benefits that 
powered exoskeleton devices might impart. This technology has 
also demonstrated an impact on psychological well-being by im -
proving quality of life and decreasing anxiety and depressive 
symptoms. Maximizing the benefits of these devices is accom -
plished by combining the device use with other established tech -
niques, including exercise, gait training, and functional electrical 
stimulation.
Ambulation Aids
A variety of ambulation aids are available to increase the area of 
support during standing and ambulation based on the individual 
needs of the patient.72,75 The type of aid provided should be based 
on the severity of the impairment and the extent of difficulty the 
patient has maintaining a center of gravity safely over the support 
area. Ambulation aids have the potential to enhance balance, 
widen the base of support, reduce load on one or both lower 
limbs, provide sensory feedback cues, and potentially facilitate 
propulsion.3,4,8 These aids act as an extension of the upper limb, 
and their proper use generally requires adequate upper limb 
strength and coordination. In addition, ambulation aids may  
allow the user to experience the physiological benefits of upright 
posture and walking, maneuver where a wheelchair cannot fit, 
and alert others to the special needs of the user. 23,24
Canes
Indications
Prescription of a cane is indicated for individuals with mild-
moderate balance impairment and for those who will benefit from 
a mild to moderate reduction in weight bearing on an affected 
limb.26,39,40
Types of Devices
There are several different types of canes (Fig. 13.28). The single-
point cane has a single tip that makes contact with the ground.

Several different handle styles are available, including the  
C-shaped handle and the functional flat grip handle. A quad cane 
has four points of contact with the ground and provides an in -
creased area of support compared with a single-point cane. Quad 
canes come in narrow- and wide-based forms for different degrees 
of support, with the wide-based style being indicated for those 
with more significant balance impairment or need for a greater 
amount of weight offloading. A visual impairment cane is a spe -
cialized type of cane with a longer shaft and red coloration on the 
distal shaft that provides sensory feedback to the user through 
both sound and vibration.23,24
Fitting and Use Considerations
The cane should be measured to the level of the greater trochanter 
with the patient in an upright position. The elbow should be 
flexed approximately 20 degrees. A cane is used on the side op -
posite the lower limb requiring the support or offloading. During 
ambulation, it is advanced with the opposite lower limb.
Crutches
Indications
The prescription of crutches is generally indicated for individuals 
with mild to moderate balance impairment and for those who 
require complete offloading of a limb because of an injury or ill -
ness. Various styles of crutches are available, with the most com -
mon types mentioned next.
Types of Crutches
• Axillary: Crutch type that provides support from the axilla to 
the floor; however, these crutches are not designed for weight 
bearing through the axilla. There are several different styles of 
axillary crutches with varying upper segments to improve 
weight-bearing distribution and function.
• Forearm: Crutch type with a single tip in contact with the 
floor and a bent proximal shaft with one or two cuffs that en -
circle and support the forearm. The forearm cuffs should pivot 
to allow the patient to lean on the crutch for free use of the 
hands.
• T riceps: Crutch type with two posterior bands that help to 
mimic the action of the triceps muscle and maintain the elbow 
in an extended position.
• Platform: Crutch type that incorporates a platform trough that 
permits weight bearing through the forearm for those who are 
unable to tolerate weight transmission through the hand.
Fitting and Use Considerations
Various gait patterns can be used with different crutch types de -
pending on the impairment and the extent of limb offloading that 
is required.28,38 T wo- or four-point gait patterns can be used when 
the balance impairment is mild to moderate and when complete 
offloading of the limb is not required. A three-point gait pattern 
can be used to provide greater to full offloading of a single limb. 
A swing-through gait pattern can be used when both limbs are 
affected and braced or during complete offloading of one limb. 
The length of the axillary crutch should be measured as the dis -
tance from the anterior axillary fold to a point 6 inches lateral to 
the fifth toe with the patient standing with the shoulders relaxed. 
The hand piece should be measured with the patient’s elbow 
flexed 30 degrees, the wrist in maximal extension, and the fingers 
forming a fist. Patients using axillary crutches should be reminded 
to not allow direct pressure or weight bearing through the axilla 
to avoid compressive radial neuropathies or plexopathies.
Walker
Indications
The prescription of a walker is indicated for individuals with 
moderate to severe balance impairment and for those who will 
benefit from a moderate to high level of weight-bearing reduction 
on an affected limb.23,24,42,87
Types of Walkers
• Hemiwalker: This walker type is indicated for those who re -
quire the stability of a walker but have unilateral weakness or 
other upper extremity impairments that prevent bilateral con -
trol of the walker.
• Standard: This walker type is indicated for those who require 
the greatest amount of stability and support for ambulation. 
This walker type requires the user to be able to lift the walker 
to advance forward. Gait is slowed with the use of a step-to gait 
pattern.
• Front wheeled: This walker type incorporates wheels on the front 
two posts to facilitate a step-through gait pattern and faster gait 
speeds. Glides can be placed on the rear uprights so the user no 
longer must pick up the walker to advance the device forward.
• Platform: This walker type typically involves the addition of 
forearm troughs to a front-wheeled walker base to facilitate 
weight bearing through the forearms instead of the hands.
• Four wheeled/Rollator: Wheels are located on all four up -
rights, which allows for a step-through gait pattern. The front 
wheels may swivel to facilitate turning and maneuvering in 
tight spaces. This type of walker typically includes a seat for 
rest breaks during ambulation and hand brakes for greater se -
curity for stopping and during sit-to-stand activities.
Fitting and Use Considerations
Walkers generally provide added support and security compared 
with canes and crutches, with the tradeoff being a slower gait. 
This is especially true for walkers that do not have wheels. The 
proper height of the walker is determined by having the patient 
stand upright with the shoulders relaxed and the elbows flexed  
20 degrees.23,24 The front of the walker should be maintained ap-
proximately 12 inches in front of the patient during standing and 
ambulation activities.
Pediatric Orthoses
Although pediatric lower extremity orthoses look and function 
similar to their adult counterparts, they are in reality completely 
different. A complex set of pathologies coupled with the charac -
teristics of physical growth in a child present additional challenge. 
A full multidisciplinary team is needed to address the complexi -
ties with this patient group. Essential to the evaluation and treat -
ment planning are parents who see the day-to-day changes in 
their children and who are willing to bring them to the many 
appointments needed for physician, therapy, and orthotic inter -
ventions. Pediatric orthoses are separated into two categories with 
overlapping groupings of diagnoses: orthopedic problems and 
neuromuscular problems.
Orthopedic problems can range from fracture management  
to bilateral hip dysplasia ( Fig. 13.29 ) and congenital talipes

Cerebral Palsy
International experts have defined cerebral palsy (CP) as being a 
group of permanent but not unchanging disorders of movement, 
posture, and motor function, which are caused by a nonprogressive 
interference, lesion, or abnormality of the developing/immature 
brain. It is not an etiological diagnosis but a clinically descriptive 
term. It is the most common cause of severe physical disability in 
childhood, with an overall incidence between 1.5 and 3 children 
per 1000.
Spastic diplegia is the most common form of CP and accounts 
for 35% of those affected. It is caused by damage to immature 
oligodendroglia, and both motor corticospinal and thalamocorti-
cal pathways are affected. Fortunately, those children with spastic 
diplegia have normal cognitive function and good prognosis for 
independent ambulation. In cases with more severe periventricu -
lar leukomalacia, it is possible for spastic quadriplegia to occur. 
These children often have significant functional limitations, cog -
nitive deficit, epilepsy, visual impairment, and are unlikely to 
obtain independent ambulation. Spastic hemiplegia is a subtype 
of CP often seen in infants born at term gestations with a history 
of in utero or perinatal stroke who usually go on to have normal 
cognition, high-level functional abilities, and independent ambu-
lation.2,54,70,73
Selective motor control enables agile and independent control 
of joint motion. Children with CP have impaired selective motor 
control, which typically occurs in combination with muscle weak-
ness, spasticity, and short muscle-tendon length. This leads in 
involuntarily coupled movement or joint torques.13
Early diagnosis of CP is essential so that interventions can be 
initiated to optimize their impact on the developing brain’s neu-
roplasticity. Multiple classification systems have been developed 
to standardize ways to assess support and therapy needs. Therapy 
is the mainstay of treatment. Physical therapy exercises are de -
signed to improve balance, postural control, and gait and to assist 
with mobility and transfers. From an occupational therapy stand-
point, the focus should be improving fine motor function of the 
upper extremities to assist the patient in performing activities of 
daily living (ADLs).2,54
Contracture is a common complication in children with spastic 
CP , and physical therapy is required for prevention. Serial casting 
may be useful in reducing or eliminating early moderate contrac -
tures. Another mainstay of treatment is botulinum toxin injection 
to help with focal spasticity. In addition to joint contractures, 
other common orthopedic problems in CP include hip dysplasia, 
scoliosis, rotational deformities (including femoral anteversion and 
tibial torsion), patella maltracking, and foot deformities (including 
equinovarus and equinovalgus). Required orthoses may include 
shoe inserts ( Fig. 13.31A), AFOs (see Fig. 13.31B), KAFOs (see 
Fig. 13.31C), HKAFOs, lumbar, thoracolumbosacral, and hand 
splints. For severe cases of CP and contractures, orthopedic surger-
ies or dorsal rhizotomy may be required.
Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease
Charcot-Marie-T ooth (CMT) disease is a group of inherited het-
erogenous motor and sensory neuropathy disorders that cause 
damage to peripheral nerves in the arms and legs either directly or 
indirectly through myelin sheath destruction. It occurs in 1 in 
2500 people and is characterized by distal muscle wasting and 
weakness, sensory disturbance, hyporeflexia, and pes cavus foot 
deformity. Symptoms usually begin in the feet and legs during 
adolescence or early adulthood. CMT disease is usually slowly 
progressive, but it does not shorten the lifespan of affected indi -
viduals. There is weakness in the legs, ankles, and feet with accom-
panying loss of muscle bulk. Other signs include high foot arches, 
hammertoes (curled toes), decreased ability to run, foot drop, 
awkward or higher than normal gait, frequent tripping and falling, 
and decreased sensation or loss of feeling in the legs and feet.43,51
CMT disease is best managed with therapies and orthoses to 
assist in mobility and maintain neutral alignment and ROM. As 
the disease progresses, AFOs are commonly used to improve toe

Orthotic management is used in combination with physical and

In recent years, research has become focused on possible gene

Arthrogryposis
Arthrogryposis is a nonprogressive neuromuscular disorder often 
discovered in utero or at birth. It is a term that describes a variety 
of conditions involving multiple joint contractures or stiffness. 
The cause is unknown, and the severity can vary. The involved 
joints often have little to no degrees of ROM. Arthrogryposis 
occurs in 1 in 3000 live births in the United States, and there is 
no known cause. Once an adequate diagnosis is made, therapy 
and orthotic management are necessary to improve function and 
mobility. Common presentations are flexed upper extremities 
combined with extended lower extremities or extended upper 
extremities combined with flexed lower extremities. Distal joints 
are usually more involved than proximal. The most important 
early-on (0–3 years of age) treatment goals are to achieve and 
maintain optimal ROM. This is achieved through serial casting 
and the use of orthoses, paired with functional strengthening and 
a positioning program. It is necessary to take great care to achieve 
appropriate stretching and splinting because progressing through 
a program aggressively can cause joint capsule damage or result 
in a fracture. Rigid splinting is generally avoided in the lower 
extremities unless it is used to facilitate gait. 10 As children age 
with arthrogryposis, they often develop secondary problems re -
lated to overuse and then may require functional splinting to 
maintain and preserve ROM.
The most common orthotic management for arthrogryposis 
for upper extremities are WHOs to improve neutral alignment for 
improved function, elbow immobilizers to achieve improved ex -
tension for functional reach, ADLs, and support during swing 
phase in gait. Lower extremity orthoses vary with severity but can 
be KAFOs, AFOs, or knee immobilizers, either in isolation or 
combination. Additionally, a TLSO may be needed to maintain 
trunk and spinal alignment to improve posture, breath support, 
and gastrointestinal motility. In the most severe cases, surgical 
correction may be needed.
Positional Contractures
Positional contractures refer to a group of conditions in which 
there is a shortening and hardening of the muscles, tendons,

or other soft tissues around a joint, leading to rigidity and loss of 
motion of the joint.91 Contractures may occur for several reasons 
but are common in patients with increased muscle tone or spastic-
ity and/or decreased mobility, including those who are primary 
wheelchair users. For example, patients with increased muscle 
tone or spasticity often present with posturing in flexed positions. 
Upper extremities with increased tone often result in elbow, wrist, 
and finger flexion combined with shoulder internal rotation. 
Lower extremities commonly rest in hip and knee flexion com -
bined with ankle plantarflexion. Without intervention, such as 
passive stretching or positioning with orthoses, these joints can 
become rigid in a flexed position and lack full extension ROM. 
This is often common in patients who have decreased mobility or 
those who sit or use a wheelchair as their primary mode of mobil-
ity. Sitting for extended periods of time can lead to shortening of 
the hip flexor muscles, knee flexors, and plantar flexors, resulting 
in decreased ROM or contractures in hip flexion, knee flexion, 
and ankle plantarflexion. Orthoses such as AFOs and knee im -
mobilizers can be used to stretch and improve ROM or to posi -
tion and decrease the risk of further contracture. Similar orthoses 
of the upper extremities, such as WHOs and elbow immobilizers, 
may also be used for dynamic mobility or as resting splints to 
maintain more neutral alignment.91 Orthoses can be made to of -
fer dynamic joint stretching. This is often done with nighttime 
AFOs for the ankle as well as dynamic knee immobilizers for the 
knees. Similar upper extremity dynamic orthoses exist to stretch 
the elbow. In cases of severe positional contractures, serial casting 
or surgery may also be appropriate.
Idiopathic Club Foot
Congenital talipes equinovarus, or idiopathic club foot, affects 
nearly 1 to 2 infants per 1000 live births and is the most com -
mon serious musculoskeletal birth defect in the world. It is seen 
in higher rates in males and can be bilateral in up to 50% of 
those affected. Idiopathic club foot is a separate entity from the 
benign positional leg and foot anomalies that are commonly 
caused by third-trimester crowding, resolve on their own, and 
include metatarsus adductus, calcaneovalgus, and positional  
cavovarus.52,91
The primary cause of idiopathic club foot is Achilles tendon 
shortening. Four key components of the deformity exist: a high 
arch, the forefoot being turned in, the heel being turned in, and 
the hind foot being pointed downward. These deformities cannot 
be corrected with gentle manipulation, and the affected foot and 
calf may be slightly smaller than the nonaffected side. The main -
stay of treatment is the Ponseti method, which involves two 
stages. The first stage involves casting, and the goal is to correct 
the foot deformity. The first cast is placed 1 to 2 weeks after  
birth, and casts must be changed every 7 to 10 days for a total  
of four or five casts. Before placement of the last cast, a tenotomy 
of the Achilles tendon is performed. The last cast is worn for  
2 to 3 weeks. The second, or bracing, stage is initiated at this 
point. Ponseti bracing is aimed at maintaining the correction 
obtained in the casting stage. The baby is fit in a supramalleolar 
orthosis with a bar (see Fig. 13.30 ). This orthosis is worn for  
23 hours a day for the first 2 months and then 12 hours a day 
until kindergarten age. If the Ponseti method is strictly adhered 
to, there is a 90% to 100% initial correction rate.12,52,91 Although 
recurrence is rare, it usually begins with a tightening of the heel 
cord. This leads to an in-toeing gait with increased adduction of 
the forefoot, varus of the heel, and loss of heel strike. 52
Genu Recurvatum
Genu recurvatum refers to a condition in which the tibiofemo -
ral joint extends beyond neutral or into knee hyperextension 
greater than 5 degrees. 19 Children presenting with genu recurva -
tum may exhibit knee pain, have an extension gait pattern, and 
have poor control during the terminal stance phase of gait. The 
posterior structures of the knee are also often placed under in -
creased stress with this condition. T reatment approaches are 
dependent on the severity of the hyperextension but consist of 
strengthening of the quadriceps muscles to improve control, us -
ing a splint or brace to block knee extension past neutral and 
maintain slight flexion, or using an AFO to improve control. 
AFOs are the most common orthosis used with genu recurva -
tum gait because the long lever arm works to maintain control 
over the knee when the stance phase begins, lessening the hyper -
extension moment. 37
Congenital Hip Dysplasia
Hip dysplasia occurs when the hip joint does not develop properly, 
and the acetabulum is too shallow. This allows the femoral head to 
slip partially or completely out of joint. Dysplasia can range from 
mild abnormalities to complete dislocation of the hip. If left un -
treated, hip dysplasia can lead to irreversible pain and loss of function 
later in life caused by increased loads being applied to an abnormally 
small surface area. Outside of the newborn stage, other indicators of 
hip dysplasia include leg length discrepancy, uneven skin folds on the 
thighs, and decreased mobility and flexion on one side.
The goal of hip dysplasia treatment is to obtain and maintain 
a concentric reduction of the femoral head into the acetabulum to 
allow for continued normal development. Different ages of pa -
tients require different kinds of treatment. Infants up to 6 months 
of age with confirmed instability or dislocation are initially treated 
using a Pavlik harness (Fig. 13.32), which is used to hold the hips 
in a position of flexion and abduction. This allows for centering 
of the femoral head in the acetabulum. 7,66,88
If the Pavlik harness fails to correct the dysplasia, many times 
infants are placed in an Ilfeld orthosis ( Fig. 13.33), which allows 
the hips to be held more rigidly in abduction. Older infants  
must often undergo closed reduction with the hip placed in 90 to 
100 degrees of flexion and the minimal amount of abduction 
needed to maintain a stable hip reduction. They are then placed 
in a spica cast for healing. After 12 months of age, into adoles -
cence, children diagnosed with hip dysplasia must undergo open 
surgical reduction and repair to ensure ongoing normal gait and 
function and to avoid arthritis later in life. 7,66
Muscular Dystrophy
Muscular dystrophy is a broad term that refers to a group of dis -
orders caused by multiple different genes, which leads to damage 
of the integrity of muscle fibers throughout the body. This dam -
age can be profound and lead to muscle degeneration, muscle fi -
ber death and phagocytosis, fiber branching and splitting, and 
eventual replacement of muscle tissue with fibrous tissue and fat. 
These changes lead to progressive weakness and loss of muscle 
mass. Initial symptoms often progress and lead to further compli-
cations, such as trouble walking without assistance, difficulty us -
ing one’s arms, joint contractures, difficulty breathing, scoliosis, 
heart problems caused by inefficiency of cardiac muscle, and 
swallowing problems.16,53,67

T reatment is largely focused on medications and therapy. Ther-
apy is focused on ROM, stretching, and low-impact aerobic exer-
cise. Bracing can be used to keep muscles and tendons stretched 
and flexible to slow progression of contractures. Braces are also used 
to aid in mobility and function by supporting weakened muscles.

will progress to a stage in which they depend on mobility aids and 
will require assistance with breathing and oxygen support.

Summary
Lower limb orthoses play an integral role in the treatment of 
many conditions of the lower extremity of a neurologic, orthope-
dic, and congenital nature.  Allowing both Adult and Pediatric 
patients with lower extremity  pathologies to interface with the 
ground and the world around them by improving gait, reducing 
pain, protection of joints, and control of aberrant movements or 
spasticity.  People with the same diagnoses  all present differently 
and require unique solutions. This is especially true with the pe -
diatric population, as they are not to be considered small adults.  
The complexities of providing lower extremity orthoses cannot be 
understated due to the various biomechanical considerations and 
require a team with holistic view of the goal for that patient.
